subject: ap-chemistry
title: Ba-EDTA Complex Equilibrium
stimulus: The polyatomic ion EDTA$^{4-}$ can form complexes with metal ions in aqueous solutions. A complex of EDTA$^{4-}$ with Ba$^{2+}$ ion forms according to the equation: Ba$^{2+}$(aq) + EDTA$^{4-}$(aq) ⇌ Ba(EDTA)$^{2-}$(aq)  $K$ = 7.7 × 10$^7$. A 50.0 mL volume of a solution that has an EDTA$^{4-}$(aq) concentration of 0.30 M is mixed with 50.0 mL of 0.20 M Ba(NO$_3$)$_2$ to produce 100.0 mL of solution.

parts.prompt:
Considering the value of K for the reaction, determine the concentration of Ba(EDTA)$^{2-}$(aq) in the
100.0 mL of solution. Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: K = 7.7 × 10$^7$ >> 1, so the reaction goes essentially to completion. Ba$^{2+}$ is the limiting reactant. [Ba$^{2+}$]$_{initial}$ after mixing = (0.20 × 50.0) / 100.0 = 0.10 M. [EDTA$^{4-}$]$_{initial}$ after mixing = (0.30 × 50.0) / 100.0 = 0.15 M. Ba$^{2+}$ is fully consumed: [Ba(EDTA)$^{2-}$] = 0.10 M.

scoring_points.description: For identifying reaction goes to completion because K >> 1: 1 point

required_elements: K = 7.7 × 10$^7$ >> 1, so the reaction proceeds essentially to completion.
alternatives.correct_example: Because K = 7.7 × 10$^7$ >> 1, the equilibrium strongly favors products. The reaction goes essentially to completion, consuming the limiting reactant Ba$^{2+}$ completely.

wrong_examples: Saying K ≈ 1 so a significant amount of reactants remains — K = 7.7 × 10$^7$ is a very large equilibrium constant.
common_traps: K >> 1 means the forward reaction is highly favored → reaction goes to completion.

scoring_points.description: For the correct [Ba(EDTA)$^{2-}$] = 0.10 M with dilution calculation: 1 point

required_elements: [Ba$^{2+}$]$_{initial}$ after mixing = (0.20 M × 50.0 mL) / 100.0 mL = 0.10 M

wrong_examples: Using initial concentrations without accounting for dilution when mixing — must apply $M_1V_1 = M_2V_2$.
common_traps: Mixing equal volumes halves all concentrations: [X]$_{mixed}$ = [X]$_{initial}$ × (V$_1$/V$_{total}$).

parts.prompt:
The solution is diluted with distilled water to a total volume of 1.00 L. After equilibrium has been
reestablished, is the number of moles of Ba$^{2+}$(aq) present in the solution greater than, less than, or equal to
the number of moles of Ba$^{2+}$(aq) present in the original solution before it was diluted? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Greater than. Dilution shifts the equilibrium to the left (Le Chatelier's principle) — concentrations decrease, Q < K, so some Ba(EDTA)$^{2-}$ dissociates to re-establish equilibrium, producing more Ba$^{2+}$ ions. The number of moles of Ba$^{2+}$ increases.

scoring_points.description: For 'greater than' as the answer: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: Greater than. Dilution causes Q < K, shifting the equilibrium toward reactants (dissociation of Ba(EDTA)$^{2-}$), producing more Ba$^{2+}$.

wrong_examples: Less than — dilution always shifts equilibrium toward more particles, increasing dissociation.
Equal to — the additional volume increases dissociation of the complex.
common_traps: Dilution shifts equilibrium to the side with more particles (or more dissolved species). Here: Ba(EDTA)$^{2-}$ (1 species) → Ba$^{2+}$ + EDTA$^{4-}$ (2 species) — more particle side is products direction.

scoring_points.description: For Le Chatelier/Q reasoning for direction of shift: 1 point

required_elements: Dilution decreases all concentrations, so Q < K.
The equilibrium shifts to the right... wait: the complex dissociates (shifts left relative to complex formation, or right relative to dissociation reaction) to re-establish K.
More Ba$^{2+}$ is produced as Ba(EDTA)$^{2-}$ partially dissociates.
alternatives.correct_example: Upon dilution, concentrations of all species decrease. For the complex formation reaction, Q = [Ba(EDTA)$^{2-}$]/([Ba$^{2+}$][EDTA$^{4-}$]). Dilution decreases the numerator and denominator equally in a 1:2 ratio... specifically Q decreases below K, shifting equilibrium to the product side (more complex). BUT for the dissociation, Q < K (for the dissociation constant = 1/K), favoring dissociation → more Ba$^{2+}$ is produced. The number of moles of Ba$^{2+}$ increases.

wrong_examples: Saying moles stay the same — in equilibrium with large but finite K, dilution always slightly increases dissociation.
common_traps: Le Chatelier: dilution shifts the equilibrium toward greater number of dissolved particles to compensate for decreased concentrations.
